mcqs.question: What is required to achieve runtime polymorphism in C++ when using base class pointers to refer to derived class objects?

options.text: The base class must have a constructor
Functions must be declared as virtual to enable dynamic binding
The derived class must use the 'override' keyword for all functions
Both classes must have exactly the same member variables

mcqs.explanation: Virtual functions enable dynamic binding, allowing the correct function to be called at runtime based on the actual object type.

options.text: Base
Derived
Compilation Error
Runtime Error

mcqs.explanation: Since show() is virtual, runtime polymorphism resolves the call to Derived::show() through the vtable.

mcqs.question: In C++, how does the compiler typically implement virtual functions internally?

options.text: Using inline expansion at compile time
Using a virtual table (vtable) and a virtual pointer (vptr) in each object
Using recursive function calls
Using static linking of function addresses

mcqs.explanation: The vtable is an array of function pointers, and each object with virtual functions contains a vptr pointing to its class vtable.

mcqs.question: Why is it recommended to declare a virtual destructor in a base class when it has virtual functions?

options.text: To allow the base class to be instantiated directly
To ensure proper cleanup of derived class resources when deleting through a base pointer
To prevent slicing of derived class objects
To enable multiple inheritance without conflicts

mcqs.explanation: Without a virtual destructor, deleting a derived object through a base pointer causes undefined behavior, often leading to memory leaks.

mcqs.question: What is the 'slicing' problem in C++?

options.text: When a derived class pointer is assigned to a base class pointer
When a derived object is assigned to a base object by value, losing derived class parts
When virtual function calls are resolved at compile time
When the vtable gets corrupted during runtime

mcqs.explanation: Slicing occurs when an object is passed by value, copying only the base portion and discarding the derived portion along with its overridden functions.

mcqs.question: What happens when a class contains at least one pure virtual function?

options.text: The class becomes a concrete class that can be instantiated
The class becomes abstract and cannot be instantiated
The compiler automatically generates a default constructor
Virtual functions are disabled for that class

mcqs.explanation: A class with a pure virtual function is abstract, meaning it cannot be instantiated but can serve as an interface for derived classes.
Both B and C override f(), so dynamic binding calls B::f() (prints 2) and C::f() (prints 3) respectively.

options.text: ShapeShape
CircleSquare
Compilation Error
Runtime Error due to pure virtual function call

mcqs.explanation: The pointer type is Shape*, but the actual objects are Circle and Square; virtual dispatch calls the appropriate derived draw() methods.
